- deep-research: Deep research harness ŌĆö fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly ŌĆö if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; highŌåÆmax: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only ŌĆö it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK ŌĆö model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER ŌĆö read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" ŌĆö whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) ŌĆö never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named ŌĆö don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items ŌĆö
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components ŌĆö for example runes and moonstones ŌĆö are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level Ōēź 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (Ōēź 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level Ōēź 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes ŌåÆ 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes ŌåÆ 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes ŌåÆ 100 G base premium (no block ŌĆö block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes ŌåÆ 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ŌØō Hmm ŌĆö "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone ŌåÆ 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones ŌåÆ 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ŌØō A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) ŌåÆ policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) ŌåÆ 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO ŌåÆ loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 ŌåÆ high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 ŌåÆ no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G ŌåÆ
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G ŌåÆ payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G ŌåÆ payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G ŌåÆ payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 ŌłÆ 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G ŌåÆ payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 ŌłÆ 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G ŌåÆ payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 ŌłÆ 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ŌØō What if a customer brings two of the same item ŌĆö one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords ŌåÆ insurance sum 2000 G (= 2├Ś1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries ŌåÆ each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) ŌåÆ the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet ŌåÆ insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) ŌåÆ cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) ŌåÆ insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3├Ś250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim ŌåÆ payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim ŌåÆ payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G ŌåÆ final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G ŌåÆ final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list ŌåÆ premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) ŌåÆ the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) ŌåÆ the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` ŌåÆ the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ŌØō Wait ŌĆö does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment ŌłÆ 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance ŌłÆ 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract ŌĆö each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### JSON Schema (normative)
237	
238	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240	
241	**Input (stdin):**
242	
243	```json
244	{
245	  "type": "object",
246	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247	  "properties": {
248	    "customer": {
249	      "type": "object",
250	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251	      "properties": {
252	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253	      }
254	    },
255	    "steps": {
256	      "type": "array",
257	      "items": {
258	        "oneOf": [
259	          {
260	            "description": "Quote step",
261	            "required": ["op", "items"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
264	              "items": {
265	                "type": "array",
266	                "items": {
267	                  "type": "object",
268	                  "required": ["type"],
269	                  "properties": {
270	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
272	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274	                  }
275	                }
276	              }
277	            }
278	          },
279	          {
280	            "description": "Claim step",
281	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282	            "properties": {
283	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285	              "incident": {
286	                "type": "object",
287	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288	                "properties": {
289	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
290	                  "damages": {
291	                    "type": "array",
292	                    "items": {
293	                      "type": "object",
294	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295	                      "properties": {
296	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298	                      }
299	                    }
300	                  }
301	                }
302	              }
303	            }
304	          }
305	        ]
306	      }
307	    }
308	  }
309	}
310	```
311	
312	**Output (stdout):**
313	
314	```json
315	{
316	  "type": "object",
317	  "required": ["results"],
318	  "properties": {
319	    "results": {
320	      "type": "array",
321	      "items": {
322	        "oneOf": [
323	          {
324	            "description": "Quote result",
325	            "required": ["premium"],
326	            "properties": {
327	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328	            }
329	          },
330	          {
331	            "description": "Claim result",
332	            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333	            "properties": {
334	              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335	              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336	            }
337	          }
338	        ]
339	      }
340	    }
341	  }
342	}
343	```
344	
345	### Schema example
346	
347	Stdin:
348	
349	```json
350	{
351	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352	  "steps": [
353	    {
354	      "op": "quote",
355	      "items": [
356	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357	      ]
358	    },
359	    {
360	      "op": "claim",
361	      "policy": 0,
362	      "incident": {
363	        "cause": "fire",
364	        "damages": [
365	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366	        ]
367	      }
368	    }
369	  ]
370	}
371	```
372	
373	Stdout shape:
374	
375	```json
376	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377	```
378	
379	## Expected Output Files
380	
381	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382	- Test files
383	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384	
385	## Constraints
386	
387	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
388	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
391

# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items ŌĆö
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components ŌĆö for example runes and moonstones ŌĆö are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level Ōēź 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (Ōēź 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level Ōēź 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes ŌåÆ 50 G base premium
- 3 runes ŌåÆ 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes ŌåÆ 100 G base premium (no block ŌĆö block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes ŌåÆ 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ŌØō Hmm ŌĆö "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone ŌåÆ 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones ŌåÆ 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ŌØō A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) ŌåÆ policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) ŌåÆ 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO ŌåÆ loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 ŌåÆ high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 ŌåÆ no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G ŌåÆ
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G ŌåÆ payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G ŌåÆ payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G ŌåÆ payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 ŌłÆ 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G ŌåÆ payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 ŌłÆ 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G ŌåÆ payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 ŌłÆ 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ŌØō What if a customer brings two of the same item ŌĆö one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords ŌåÆ insurance sum 2000 G (= 2├Ś1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries ŌåÆ each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) ŌåÆ the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet ŌåÆ insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) ŌåÆ cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) ŌåÆ insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3├Ś250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim ŌåÆ payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim ŌåÆ payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G ŌåÆ final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G ŌåÆ final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list ŌåÆ premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) ŌåÆ the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) ŌåÆ the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` ŌåÆ the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ŌØō Wait ŌĆö does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment ŌłÆ 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance ŌłÆ 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract ŌĆö each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### JSON Schema (normative)

The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.

**Input (stdin):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
  "properties": {
    "customer": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
      "properties": {
        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
      }
    },
    "steps": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote step",
            "required": ["op", "items"],
            "properties": {
              "op": { "const": "quote" },
              "items": {
                "type": "array",
                "items": {
                  "type": "object",
                  "required": ["type"],
                  "properties": {
                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim step",
            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
            "properties": {
              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
              "incident": {
                "type": "object",
                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
                "properties": {
                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
                  "damages": {
                    "type": "array",
                    "items": {
                      "type": "object",
                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
                      "properties": {
                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
                      }
                    }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Output (stdout):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["results"],
  "properties": {
    "results": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote result",
            "required": ["premium"],
            "properties": {
              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim result",
            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
            "properties": {
              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

input.prompt:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office (insurance quote + claim processing for magical items)
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Requirements (from Example Mapping of the kata spec in prompt.md):

The system processes a scenario: a `customer` object ({yearsWithMHPCO: integer}) and a `steps` array. Each step is either a quote (op: "quote", items: [{type, material?, enchantment?, cursed?}]) or a claim (op: "claim", policy: <zero-based index of quote step>, incident: {cause, damages: [{itemType, amount}]}). Output is {results: [...]} with each quote result = {premium: integer} and each claim result = {payout: integer, remainingCap: integer}.

QUOTE RULES:
- Base premiums: sword 100 G (value 1000), amulet 60 G (600), staff 80 G (800), potion 40 G (400). Components (rune, moonstone) 25 G each (value 250).
- Building block: exactly 3 alike components (same type) ŌåÆ 60 G base premium for that block. Examples: 2 runes ŌåÆ 50; 3 runes ŌåÆ 60; 4 runes ŌåÆ 100 (no block, block requires exactly 3); 7 runes ŌåÆ 175; 2 runes + 1 moonstone ŌåÆ 75 (different types, no block); 3 runes + 3 moonstones ŌåÆ 120 (two blocks).
- Item-specific modifiers (applied per affected item's base premium): cursed ŌåÆ +50%; enchantment Ōēź 5 ŌåÆ +30%.
- Policy-wide modifiers (applied to sum of item base premiums): yearsWithMHPCO Ōēź 2 ŌåÆ ŌłÆ20% loyalty discount; first insurance ŌåÆ +10% surcharge (applies to EVERY quote ŌĆö each item/quote is treated as a first insurance regardless of history); each contract after the customer's first quote in the scenario ŌåÆ ŌłÆ15% follow-up discount.
- Modifier percentages are computed off the (policy) base premium and added/subtracted as flat amounts (e.g. 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench ŌłÆ 20 loyalty + 10 first ŌłÆ 15 follow-up = 155).
- Processing fee: +5 G added at the very end.
- Rounding: final premium rounded UP to whole G (in MHPCO's favor); intermediates kept as fractions.
- Empty item list ŌåÆ premium 5 G (just the fee).
- Unknown item type (e.g. "broomstick") ŌåÆ error (the library should throw / signal an error; the CLI exits non-zero, writes error to stderr, no results to stdout).

Integration examples:
- Newcomer (0 years), cursed steel sword ench 3 ŌåÆ 165 G (100 + 50 curse + 10 first = 160 + 5 fee).
- 3-year customer, second quote in scenario, cursed steel sword ench 7 ŌåÆ 160 G (100 + 50 + 30 ŌłÆ 20 loyalty + 10 first ŌłÆ 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee).

CLAIM RULES:
- A quote step creates a policy. Insurance sum = sum of items' unmodified insurance values (block discount doesn't change it). Payout cap = 2 ├Ś insurance sum, tracked across successive claims on the same policy.
- Per damage entry: enchantment Ōēź 8 of the damaged item ŌåÆ reimburse 50% of damage amount; dragon material ŌåÆ full reimbursement (if both apply, the 50% rule wins... actually per spec: dragon ench 9, 1000 damage ŌåÆ 400 payout: both clauses apply, 50% rule wins, then deductible 500ŌłÆ100). Otherwise full reimbursement.
- 100 G deductible per damage entry (per damaged item).
- Examples: regular steel sword ench 3, damage 500 ŌåÆ 400; rune damage 200 ŌåÆ 100; dragon sword ench 8, damage 1000 ŌåÆ 400; dragon sword ench 5, damage 800 ŌåÆ 700; steel sword ench 9, damage 1000 ŌåÆ 400; sword 500 + amulet 300 damaged ŌåÆ 600 (deductible once per item).
- Two swords insured ŌåÆ sum 2000, cap 4000; two sword damage entries ŌåÆ each its own deductible.
- Cap: sword policy (cap 2000), two claims of 1500: first ŌåÆ payout 1400, remaining 600; second ŌåÆ desired 1400 reduced to 600, remaining 0. remainingCap reported per claim result.
- Final payout rounded DOWN to whole G.
- Errors (CLI non-zero exit + stderr): damage entry for item not in policy (or unknown type); more damage entries of a type than items of that type covered; negative damage amount.

CLI (to be implemented at src/cli.ts eventually): reads scenario JSON from stdin, writes {results} JSON to stdout. The core test list should focus on a pure function processing a scenario object (e.g. processScenario(scenario) ŌåÆ {results}) plus error throwing; CLI wiring tested separately/minimally.

Order tests simple ŌåÆ complex: start with empty quote (fee only), single item base premiums, components, block, item modifiers, policy modifiers, integration premiums, then claims (standard, special clauses, deductible per item, cap tracking, multi-claim), then errors.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

**Base Functionality Tests**: 34, ordered simple ŌåÆ complex

1. Quotes, basics (6): empty item list ŌåÆ 5; single sword ŌåÆ 115, amulet ŌåÆ 71, staff ŌåÆ 93, potion ŌåÆ 49 (all include the always-applied +10% first-insurance and +5 fee); one result per step
2. Quotes, components and block (7): 1 rune ŌåÆ 33 (demonstrates round-up), 2 runes ŌåÆ 60, 3 runes (block) ŌåÆ 71, 4 runes ŌåÆ 115, 7 runes ŌåÆ 198, 2 runes + 1 moonstone ŌåÆ 88, 3 runes + 3 moonstones ŌåÆ 137
3. Quotes, item modifiers (2): cursed +50% (ŌåÆ 165), enchantment Ōēź 5 +30% (ŌåÆ 145)
4. Quotes, policy modifiers (2): loyalty ŌłÆ20% for 2+ years (ŌåÆ 95), follow-up ŌłÆ15% on later quotes (ŌåÆ 100)
5. Quotes, integration examples (2): newcomer cursed sword ench 3 ŌåÆ 165; 3-year customer, second quote, cursed sword ench 7 ŌåÆ 160
6. Claims, payouts (6): standard 500 ŌåÆ 400; rune 200 ŌåÆ 100; ench Ōēź 8 ŌåÆ 50% rule (1000 ŌåÆ 400); dragon material full reimbursement (800 ŌåÆ 700); both clauses ŌåÆ 50% wins (1000 ŌåÆ 400); payout rounded down (999 ŌåÆ 399)
7. Claims, deductible per item (2): sword + amulet ŌåÆ 600; two insured swords, separate deductibles per damage entry
8. Claims, payout cap (3): remainingCap = 2├Ś insurance sum ŌłÆ payout (1500 ŌåÆ 1400/600); later claim capped (1500 ŌåÆ 600/0); cap from unmodified insurance values (two swords ŌåÆ 4000)
9. Errors (4): unknown item type; damage for uncovered item type; more damage entries than covered items of that type; negative damage amount

**Not included** (beyond the Example Mapping spec, save for later):
- CLI wiring (src/cli.ts stdin/stdout/exit codes) ŌĆö tested separately/minimally after the core is done
- Any input-shape validation or generic edge cases not called for by the spec

**Expected values note**: All quote premiums include the +10% first-insurance surcharge (applies to every quote per spec) and the +5 fee, with final round-up; payouts use the 100 G per-entry deductible with final round-down.

**Next Step**: Launch the `red` agent to activate the first test: "should return premium 5 for a quote with an empty item list (processing fee only)". Note for the Red phase: `src/claim-office.ts` does not exist yet, so the first activation will fail at module resolution/compilation, and devDependencies must be installed with `NODE_ENV=development pnpm install` (the environment's NODE_ENV=production skips them ŌĆö already installed now).

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Report (final holistic pass)

**Files:**
- Implementation: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-06-10_21-42-37_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_fable-5-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- Tests: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-06-10_21-42-37_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_fable-5-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`
- Done marker: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-06-10_21-42-37_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_fable-5-no-thinking/experiment-done.txt`

**Naming evaluation (first priority):**
- Renamed `calculateTotalBasePremium` to `totalBasePremium` ŌĆö the `calculate` verb prefix was redundant and inconsistent with sibling noun-phrase aggregates (`totalItemSurcharge`, `incidentPayout`, `policyDiscountRate`). Local in `processQuoteStep` renamed to `basePremium` to avoid shadowing.
- All other names (`payoutForDamage`, `payoutCapForItems`, `reimbursementRateForItem`, `assertDamagesWithinCoverage`, the negative-amount guard's error message) accurately describe current behavior across all 34 tests. Kept.

**Refactorings applied (one at a time, tests run after each):**
1. Rename for naming consistency (Rule 2). Tests: 34/34 green.
2. Extracted `isClaimStep` type guard (`step is ClaimStep => "incident" in step`) and used it in `processScenario`, replacing the anonymous structural check `"incident" in step` at the dispatch site (Rule 2: names the decision being made). Tests: 34/34 green.

**Evaluated and deliberately NOT changed:**
- Rule 3: `INSURANCE_VALUES` is numerically `BASE_PREMIUMS x 10` for every item type. This is coincidental alignment of two independent domain facts (pricing vs. insured value), not shared knowledge ŌĆö deriving one from the other would encode a false invariant. Rule 2 trumps Rule 3 here.
- The `steps[step.policy] as QuoteStep` cast in `processClaimStep`: replacing it with a runtime guard would introduce untested error paths (invalid policy index), violating TDD minimalism.
- Dead-code check: no unused functions or constants. `op` and `cause` fields are unused by logic but document the input data contract; kept in the types.
- Rule 4: no unnecessary abstractions found; each helper is invoked and carries a distinct domain concept.

**APP mass (touched region: `processScenario` + `processQuoteStep` + new guard):**
36 ŌåÆ 39 (╬ö +3: one extra binding +1, one extra invocation +2). The increase is accepted because Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP ŌĆö the dispatch now reads `isClaimStep(step)` instead of a structural property probe.

**Verification:**
- `pnpm test`: 34/34 passing after every change
- `npx tsc --noEmit`: exit 0 (no `build` script exists in this project)

All tests from the list are implemented and green; `experiment-done.txt` written with `DONE` per experiment-mode instructions. TDD cycle complete.

- Be at src/cli.ts; read a scenario JSON document from stdin, call processScenario, and write `{"results": [...]}` JSON to stdout, exiting 0.
- On error (e.g. unknown item type like "broomstick", uncovered damage item, negative damage amount): exit with a non-zero status code, write an error description to stderr, and write NO results to stdout.

Test approach: spec tests should spawn the CLI as a child process with `npx tsx src/cli.ts` (tsx is a devDependency), piping JSON to stdin and capturing stdout/stderr/exit code (use node:child_process spawnSync or execFile). Keep the list MINIMAL ŌĆö this is thin wiring; 3 tests suffice:
1. quote scenario on stdin ŌåÆ stdout JSON {results:[{premium:...}]}, exit code 0 (e.g. customer 5 years, one amulet ŌåÆ premium computed by the core; a known good value: {yearsWithMHPCO: 0}, items [{type:"sword"}] ŌåÆ premium 115)
2. quote+claim scenario ŌåÆ results array with premium and {payout, remainingCap} (e.g. the schema example: 5-year customer, amulet quote then claim 200 damage ŌåÆ premium 53 (60ŌłÆ12+6+5? ŌĆö DO NOT hardcode blindly; compute via the core in the test by importing processScenario and comparing CLI output to processScenario(scenario) output, which avoids brittle constants)
3. unknown item type (broomstick) ŌåÆ non-zero exit code, stderr non-empty, stdout has no results

Note CLI spawn tests are slower (~1s each); that is acceptable.

I kept them separate because the 10x relationship is a coincidence of the current spec values, not a stated rule ŌĆö deriving one from the other would encode knowledge the spec doesn't guarantee (Rule 2: explicit tables reveal intent better than a magic multiplier).

- Naming: kept `STDIN_FD` and `scenario`; no exported function names in this script to re-evaluate.

content.text:
The MHPCO Claim Office kata is complete. All 37 tests pass (34 core + 3 CLI), the CLI works end to end, and `experiment-done.txt` is written.

**How it went:**

- **Test list phase**: The `test-list` agent created 34 `it.todo()` tests in `src/claim-office.spec.ts` covering the kata spec ŌĆö quote basics, component blocks, item/policy modifiers, integration examples, claim payouts, deductibles, payout cap tracking, and error cases. A second short list (3 tests) covered the CLI wiring in `src/cli.spec.ts`.
- **Red-Green-Refactor cycles**: Every test went through the `red` ŌåÆ `green` ŌåÆ `refactor` agent cycle. Each Red phase made explicit compilation/runtime predictions (verified correct in all but one case, where an expected compile error didn't materialize due to structural typing). 9 tests passed immediately upon activation because earlier minimal implementations had already generalized correctly (e.g., the per-item sum covered "2 runes ŌåÆ 60", and the enchantmentŌēź8 rule already satisfied the dragon-precedence test) ŌĆö Green was skipped for those, with Refactor still run.
- **Implementation** (`src/claim-office.ts`): A pure `processScenario(scenario)` function with named domain constants (base premiums, insurance values, surcharge/discount rates, block size, deductible), per-type block pricing, item-level vs. policy-level modifiers, final round-up for premiums / round-down for payouts (intermediates kept fractional), cumulative per-policy cap tracking via a `paidByPolicy` map, and validation that throws on unknown types, uncovered damage entries, over-claimed counts, and negative amounts.
- **CLI** (`src/cli.ts`): Reads JSON from stdin, writes `{"results": [...]}` to stdout, and on error prints a one-line description to stderr and exits 1 ŌĆö verified by spawning the real process in tests and by the smoke test above (schema example ŌåÆ `{"results":[{"premium":59},{"payout":100,"remainingCap":1100}]}`, broomstick ŌåÆ exit 1 with `Error: Unknown item type: broomstick`).
